invariant_definition: *Ātma-khyāti* is the Yogācāra theory of erroneous cognition: in cases like rope-snake the apparent snake is not an external entity at all but a transformation of *vijñāna* itself. There is no need to posit an external snake known elsewhere (Nyāya *anyathā-khyāti*) nor an *anirvacanīya* superimposed content (Advaita *anirvacanīya-khyāti*) nor a positive false content (Bhāṭṭa *viparīta-khyāti*): the snake-appearance is consciousness-internal, a *parikalpita* construction within the *vijñāna*-stream. *Ātma-khyāti* is one of the standard *khyāti-vāda* positions enumerated by Vedāntic doxographers (alongside *akhyāti*, *anyathā-khyāti*, *asat-khyāti*, and the rest) and is the position with which the Vedāntic schools must reckon as the Yogācāra alternative. Implicitly it generalizes: if the rope-snake snake is consciousness-internal, so is every other object — *bāhyārtha-niṣedha* is built into the doctrine.

per_school.definition: The cognition called 'snake' has *ātman* — its substrate-content — internal to *vijñāna* itself, not external. Vasubandhu's *Viṃśatikā* argues that the standard objections to *bāhyārtha-niṣedha* (dream-experience showing space-time regularity, intersubjective agreement, causal efficacy, instruction) are all met by a non-realist account. The *khyāti* in question is thus *ātma-khyāti*: appearance of the self (the *vijñāna*'s own state) misconstrued as object.

Advaita acknowledges *ātma-khyāti* in the doxography as the Yogācāra position and partially shares the move against external realism, but rejects the reduction of all appearance to *vijñāna*-transformations. The substrate of error must be a real *adhiṣṭhāna* (Brahman/rope), not a momentary *vijñāna*-stream; otherwise the asymmetry between substrate and superimposed content is lost.

*Ātma-khyāti* is rejected: the explanatory work it does can be done by *anyathā-khyāti* without abandoning realism about external objects. Vasubandhu's arguments are systematically engaged in *Nyāya-Sūtra* 4.2 and the *Nyāya-Bhāṣya*.

Rejected by both Bhāṭṭa and Prābhākara on broadly realist grounds; the rite-centered hermeneutic project requires that the objects of ritual cognition be external and stable. *Ātma-khyāti* would undermine the realist substrate Mīmāṃsā depends on.

translator_note: Leave in Sanskrit; *khyāti*-vocabulary should be preserved as a technical set. 'Self-appearance theory' is approximate but acceptable in expository prose.
